You are working on the pinterest/ktlint repository.
The codebase is at version 0.49 (commit: 87153ae1).

## Issue to Resolve

Formatting enum class with member definition and missing trailing commas causes rule violations on first run
## Expected Behavior
<!---Tell us what should happen. -->
<!-- If relevant, provide a link to the relevant section in the [Kotlin Coding Conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html) or [Android Kotlin Style Guide](https://developer.android.com/kotlin/style-guide).  -->

<!-- Note: Ktlint should not produce any code which conflict with IntelliJ default code formatting. So please verify that your expectation is accepted by IntelliJ default code formatting. -->

When formatting and validating the following enum class, the ktlint command should exit successfully (status `0`):

```kotlin
enum class TestEnum() {
    A,
    B;

    companion object {
        const val A_NUMBER = 1L
    }
}
```

## Observed Behavior
<!---Tell us what happens instead of the expected behavior -->
<!--- Provide the exact command which was executed but please -->
<!--- ensure that the flag "--log-level=debug" is added to the -->
<!--- command as well. Provide the output of this command. -->

When running ktlint on the above enum class, it is formatted correctly, but the run fails with rule violations (exit status `1`):
```bash
$ ktlint --format 
17:25:00.694 [main] INFO com.pinterest.ktlint.internal.KtlintCommandLine - Enable default patterns [**/*.kt, **/*.kts]
/home/karl/tasks/ktlint-issue-example/TestEnum.kt:1:1: Missing trailing comma before "}" (trailing-comma-on-declaration-site)
/home/karl/tasks/ktlint-issue-example/TestEnum.kt:5:4: Unnecessary semicolon (no-semi)

Summary error count (descending) by rule:
  no-semi: 1
  trailing-comma-on-declaration-site: 1
Exit status: 1 
```

I note that the line number for the `no-semi` violation is the line number after formatting.

A second run on the (now properly formatted) code runs correctly (exit status `0`):
```bash
$ ktlint --format
17:25:05.397 [main] INFO com.pinterest.ktlint.internal.KtlintCommandLine - Enable default patterns [**/*.kt, **/*.kts]
Exit status: 0
```

## Steps to Reproduce
Run command on above enum class:
```ktlint --format```

## Your Environment
<!--- Include as many relevant details about the environment you experienced the bug in -->
* Version of ktlint used: 0.48.1
* Operating System and version: Arch Linux

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

I have the same issue

See release notes of 0.48.0:
> * The default value for trailing comma's on call site is changed to `true` unless the `android codestyle` is enabled. Note that KtLint from a consistency viewpoint *enforces* the trailing comma on call site while default IntelliJ IDEA formatting only *allows* the trailing comma but leaves it up to the developer's discretion. ([#1670](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/pull/1670))

@paul-dingemans I don't quite follow. My issue is that after doing formatting, ktlint reports invalid  `no-semi` and `trailing-comma-on-declaration-site` rule violations. A subsequent run of ktlint (correctly) passes the same code.

Ah now I see. Although it is not visual in the textual representation of the code, the trailing comma is not inserted at the correct place in the AST representation of the code. I will try to fix it.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `RELEASING.md`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRule.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

content.thinking:
**Fixing ktlint issues**

I need to fix an issue with ktlint 0.49 related to formatting enums with members and companion objects. The problem is that a missing trailing comma before the closing brace '}' causes violations on the first run. It also reports issues with semicolons. I notice that Kotlin requires a semicolon between enum entries and members, but I'm unsure if it's still necessary with a trailing comma after the last entry. This is a bit tricky!**Clarifying enum formatting**

I still think a semicolon is necessary to separate enum entries like `A, B; companion object {}`. But, if there’s a trailing comma inserted before the semicolon, does that change things? The trailing comma should come after the last enum entry but before the semicolon, right? The error mentions a missing trailing comma before the closing brace '}', which could indicate a missing comma after the companion object constant. Ktlint seems like it should add this automatically when needed, as trailing commas are generally allowed in Kotlin conventions.**Understanding enum entry formatting**

I realize that the enum entries are inside the class body, not as a parameter list, and since Kotlin 1.4, it does allow trailing commas in enum entries. The semicolon is still mandatory, so I’m guessing that the no-semi violation refers to that semicolon after the entries. The warning about an unnecessary semicolon likely relates to the semicolon at the end of line 3. After formatting, if the semicolon got moved to a separate line before the companion object, it may be flagged as unnecessary and could lead to invalid code if removed!**Analyzing semicolon requirements**

I’m considering whether a trailing comma means a semicolon isn’t required. According to Kotlin grammar, if there are no members, a semicolon after enum entries is optional, but it’s mandatory when there are members. So, I can’t just remove the semicolon. The no-semi rule shouldn't flag it in enums with members. I’m thinking if there’s an issue with the AST due to the trailing comma rule causing the PSI to be out of sync and leading to incorrect error reporting. I should try running it with the formatting flag!**Investigating ktlint errors**

It seems that while the trailing-comma-on-declaration-site rule does fix issues, it still reports errors during the same run. This has me wondering if it runs in lint mode and emits violations before performing any autocorrect. Ktlint should allow for autocorrect without reporting fixed errors, but it appears to show errors regardless, likely due to an AST mismatch after the correction. The no-semi violation could also be happening due to line number changes from the autocorrect. I should definitely check the `TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRule.kt` for clarity!

  2→
  3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule
  4→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.EditorConfigProperties
  5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.UsesEditorConfigProperties
  6→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.EditorConfigProperty
  7→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType
  8→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.ARROW
  9→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.CLASS
 10→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.DESTRUCTURING_DECLARATION
 11→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.FUNCTION_LITERAL
 12→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.FUNCTION_TYPE
 13→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.TYPE_PARAMETER_LIST
 14→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.VALUE_PARAMETER_LIST
 15→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.WHEN_ENTRY
 16→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.ElementType.WHITE_SPACE
 17→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.children
 18→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.isCodeLeaf
 19→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.lineIndent
 20→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.noNewLineInClosedRange
 21→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.prevCodeLeaf
 22→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.prevLeaf
 23→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.ast.upsertWhitespaceAfterMe
 24→import kotlin.properties.Delegates
 25→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType
 26→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType.PropertyValueParser
 27→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.lang.ASTNode
 28→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.psi.PsiElement
 29→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.psi.PsiWhiteSpace
 30→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.psi.tree.TokenSet
 31→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtClass
 32→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtCollectionLiteralExpression
 33→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtDestructuringDeclaration
 34→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtEnumEntry
 35→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtFunctionLiteral
 36→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtParameterList
 37→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtPsiFactory
 38→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtValueArgumentList
 39→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtWhenEntry
 40→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.KtWhenExpression
 41→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.psiUtil.anyDescendantOfType
 42→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.psiUtil.collectDescendantsOfType
 43→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.psiUtil.nextLeaf
 44→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.psiUtil.parents
 45→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.psi.psiUtil.prevLeaf
 46→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.utils.addToStdlib.cast
 47→
 48→/**
 49→ * Linting trailing comma for declaration site.
 50→ *
 51→ * @see [Kotlin Style Guide](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html#trailing-commas)
 52→ */
 53→public class TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRule :
 54→    Rule(
 55→        id = "trailing-comma-on-declaration-site",
 56→        visitorModifiers = setOf(
 57→            VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule(
 58→                ruleId = "standard:wrapping",
 59→                loadOnlyWhenOtherRuleIsLoaded = true,
 60→                runOnlyWhenOtherRuleIsEnabled = true,
 61→            ),
 62→            VisitorModifier.RunAsLateAsPossible,
 63→        ),
 64→    ),
 65→    UsesEditorConfigProperties {
 66→
 67→    override val editorConfigProperties: List<EditorConfigProperty<*>> = listOf(
 68→        TRAILING_COMMA_ON_DECLARATION_SITE_PROPERTY,
 69→    )
 70→
 71→    private var allowTrailingComma by Delegates.notNull<Boolean>()
 72→
 73→    private fun ASTNode.isTrailingCommaAllowed() =
 74→        elementType in TYPES_ON_DECLARATION_SITE && allowTrailingComma
 75→
 76→    override fun beforeFirstNode(editorConfigProperties: EditorConfigProperties) {
 77→        allowTrailingComma = editorConfigProperties.getEditorConfigValue(TRAILING_COMMA_ON_DECLARATION_SITE_PROPERTY)
 78→    }
 79→
 80→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
 81→        node: ASTNode,
 82→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
 83→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
 84→    ) {
 85→        // Keep processing of element types in sync with Intellij Kotlin formatting settings.
 86→        // https://github.com/JetBrains/intellij-kotlin/blob/master/formatter/src/org/jetbrains/kotlin/idea/formatter/trailingComma/util.kt
 87→        when (node.elementType) {
 88→            CLASS -> visitClass(node, emit, autoCorrect)
 89→            DESTRUCTURING_DECLARATION -> visitDestructuringDeclaration(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 90→            FUNCTION_LITERAL -> visitFunctionLiteral(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 91→            TYPE_PARAMETER_LIST -> visitTypeList(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 92→            VALUE_PARAMETER_LIST -> visitValueList(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 93→            WHEN_ENTRY -> visitWhenEntry(node, autoCorrect, emit)
 94→            else -> Unit
 95→        }
 96→    }
 97→
 98→    private fun visitDestructuringDeclaration(
 99→        node: ASTNode,
100→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
101→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
102→    ) {
103→        val inspectNode = node
104→            .children()
105→            .last { it.elementType == ElementType.RPAR }
106→        node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
107→            inspectNode = inspectNode,
108→            isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
109→            autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
110→            emit = emit,
111→        )
112→    }
113→
114→    private fun visitFunctionLiteral(
115→        node: ASTNode,
116→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
117→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
118→    ) {
119→        val inspectNode = node
120→            .children()
121→            .lastOrNull { it.elementType == ARROW }
122→            ?: // lambda w/o an arrow -> no arguments -> no commas
123→            return
124→        node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
125→            inspectNode = inspectNode,
126→            isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
127→            autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
128→            emit = emit,
129→        )
130→    }
131→
132→    private fun visitValueList(
133→        node: ASTNode,
134→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
135→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
136→    ) {
137→        if (node.treeParent.elementType != FUNCTION_LITERAL) {
138→            node
139→                .children()
140→                .lastOrNull { it.elementType == ElementType.RPAR }
141→                ?.let { inspectNode ->
142→                    node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
143→                        inspectNode = inspectNode,
144→                        isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
145→                        autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
146→                        emit = emit,
147→                    )
148→                }
149→        }
150→    }
151→
152→    private fun visitTypeList(
153→        node: ASTNode,
154→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
155→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
156→    ) {
157→        val inspectNode = node
158→            .children()
159→            .first { it.elementType == ElementType.GT }
160→        node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
161→            inspectNode = inspectNode,
162→            isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
163→            autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
164→            emit = emit,
165→        )
166→    }
167→
168→    private fun visitWhenEntry(
169→        node: ASTNode,
170→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
171→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
172→    ) {
173→        val psi = node.psi
174→        require(psi is KtWhenEntry)
175→        if (psi.isElse || psi.parent.cast<KtWhenExpression>().leftParenthesis == null) {
176→            // no commas for "else" or when there are no opening parenthesis for the when-expression
177→            return
178→        }
179→
180→        val inspectNode = node
181→            .children()
182→            .first { it.elementType == ARROW }
183→        node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
184→            inspectNode = inspectNode,
185→            isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
186→            autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
187→            emit = emit,
188→        )
189→    }
190→    private fun visitClass(
191→        node: ASTNode,
192→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
193→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
194→    ) {
195→        val psi = node.psi
196→        require(psi is KtClass)
197→
198→        node
199→            .takeIf { psi.isEnum() }
200→            ?.findChildByType(ElementType.CLASS_BODY)
201→            ?.takeUnless {
202→                // Do nothing when last two entries are on same line as no trailing comma should be inserted
203→                it.lastTwoEnumEntriesAreOnSameLine()
204→            }?.let { classBody ->
205→                val nodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition = classBody.findNodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition()
206→                node.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
207→                    inspectNode = nodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition,
208→                    isTrailingCommaAllowed = node.isTrailingCommaAllowed(),
209→                    autoCorrect = autoCorrect,
210→                    emit = emit,
211→                )
212→            }
213→    }
214→
215→    /**
216→     * Determines the [ASTNode] before which the trailing comma is allowed.
217→     *
218→     * If the list of enumeration entries is terminated by a semicolon, that semicolon will be returned. Otherwise, the
219→     * last element of the class.
220→     */
221→    private fun ASTNode.findNodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition(): ASTNode {
222→        val lastEnumEntry = children().last { it.psi is KtEnumEntry }
223→
224→        val semicolonAfterLastEnumEntry = lastEnumEntry
225→            .children()
226→            .singleOrNull { it.elementType == ElementType.SEMICOLON }
227→
228→        return semicolonAfterLastEnumEntry ?: lastChildNode
229→    }
230→
231→    private fun ASTNode.lastTwoEnumEntriesAreOnSameLine(): Boolean {
232→        val lastTwoEnumEntries =
233→            children()
234→                .filter { it.psi is KtEnumEntry }
235→                .toList()
236→                .takeLast(2)
237→
238→        return lastTwoEnumEntries.count() == 2 && noNewLineInClosedRange(lastTwoEnumEntries[0], lastTwoEnumEntries[1])
239→    }
240→
241→    private fun ASTNode.reportAndCorrectTrailingCommaNodeBefore(
242→        inspectNode: ASTNode,
243→        isTrailingCommaAllowed: Boolean,
244→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
245→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
246→    ) {
247→        val prevLeaf = inspectNode.prevLeaf()
248→        val trailingCommaNode = prevLeaf?.findPreviousTrailingCommaNodeOrNull()
249→        val trailingCommaState = when {
250→            isMultiline(psi) -> if (trailingCommaNode != null) TrailingCommaState.EXISTS else TrailingCommaState.MISSING
251→            else -> if (trailingCommaNode != null) TrailingCommaState.REDUNDANT else TrailingCommaState.NOT_EXISTS
252→        }
253→        when (trailingCommaState) {
254→            TrailingCommaState.EXISTS ->
255→                if (isTrailingCommaAllowed) {
256→                    inspectNode
257→                        .treeParent
258→                        .takeIf { it.elementType == WHEN_ENTRY }
259→                        ?.findChildByType(ARROW)
260→                        ?.prevLeaf()
261→                        ?.let { lastNodeBeforeArrow ->
262→                            if (lastNodeBeforeArrow.elementType != WHITE_SPACE || !lastNodeBeforeArrow.textContains('\n')) {
263→                                emit(
264→                                    trailingCommaNode!!.startOffset,
265→                                    "Expected a newline between the trailing comma and  \"${inspectNode.text}\"",
266→                                    true,
267→                                )
268→                                if (autoCorrect) {
269→                                    val parentIndent = "\n" + inspectNode.getWhenEntryIndent()
270→                                    lastNodeBeforeArrow.upsertWhitespaceAfterMe(parentIndent)
271→                                }
272→                            }
273→                        }
274→                } else {
275→                    emit(
276→                        trailingCommaNode!!.startOffset,
277→                        "Unnecessary trailing comma before \"${inspectNode.text}\"",
278→                        true,
279→                    )
280→                    if (autoCorrect) {
281→                        this.removeChild(trailingCommaNode)
282→                    }
283→                }
284→            TrailingCommaState.MISSING ->
285→                if (isTrailingCommaAllowed) {
286→                    val addNewLineBeforeArrowInWhenEntry = addNewLineBeforeArrowInWhen()
287→                    val prevNode = inspectNode.prevCodeLeaf()!!
288→                    if (addNewLineBeforeArrowInWhenEntry) {
289→                        emit(
290→                            prevNode.startOffset + prevNode.textLength,
291→                            "Missing trailing comma and newline before \"${inspectNode.text}\"",
292→                            true,
293→                        )
294→                    } else {
295→                        emit(
296→                            prevNode.startOffset + prevNode.textLength,
297→                            "Missing trailing comma before \"${inspectNode.text}\"",
298→                            true,
299→                        )
300→                    }
301→                    if (autoCorrect) {
302→                        if (addNewLineBeforeArrowInWhenEntry) {
303→                            val parentIndent = "\n" + prevNode.getWhenEntryIndent()
304→                            val leafBeforeArrow = (psi as KtWhenEntry).arrow?.prevLeaf()
305→                            if (leafBeforeArrow != null && leafBeforeArrow is PsiWhiteSpace) {
306→                                val newLine = KtPsiFactory(prevNode.psi).createWhiteSpace(parentIndent)
307→                                leafBeforeArrow.replace(newLine)
308→                            } else {
309→                                val newLine = KtPsiFactory(prevNode.psi).createWhiteSpace(parentIndent)
310→                                prevNode.psi.parent.addAfter(newLine, prevNode.psi)
311→                            }
312→                        }
313→
314→                        if (inspectNode.treeParent.elementType == ElementType.ENUM_ENTRY) {
315→                            with(KtPsiFactory(prevNode.psi)) {
316→                                val parentIndent =
317→                                    (prevNode.psi.parent.prevLeaf() as? PsiWhiteSpace)?.text
318→                                        ?: "\n${prevNode.lineIndent()}"
319→                                val newline = createWhiteSpace(parentIndent)
320→                                val enumEntry = inspectNode.treeParent.psi
321→                                enumEntry.apply {
322→                                    add(newline)
323→                                    removeChild(inspectNode)
324→                                    parent.addAfter(createSemicolon(), this)
325→                                }
326→                            }
327→                        }
328→
329→                        val comma = KtPsiFactory(prevNode.psi).createComma()
330→                        prevNode.psi.parent.addAfter(comma, prevNode.psi)
331→                    }
332→                }
333→            TrailingCommaState.REDUNDANT -> {
334→                emit(
335→                    trailingCommaNode!!.startOffset,
336→                    "Unnecessary trailing comma before \"${inspectNode.text}\"",
337→                    true,
338→                )
339→                if (autoCorrect) {
340→                    this.removeChild(trailingCommaNode)
341→                }
342→            }
343→            TrailingCommaState.NOT_EXISTS -> Unit
344→        }
345→    }
346→
347→    private fun ASTNode.getWhenEntryIndent() =
348→        // The when entry can be a simple value but might also be a complex expression.
349→        parents()
350→            .last { it.elementType == WHEN_ENTRY }
351→            .lineIndent()
352→
353→    private fun isMultiline(element: PsiElement): Boolean = when {
354→        element.parent is KtFunctionLiteral -> isMultiline(element.parent)
355→        element is KtFunctionLiteral -> containsLineBreakInLeavesRange(element.valueParameterList!!, element.arrow!!)
356→        element is KtWhenEntry -> containsLineBreakInLeavesRange(element.firstChild, element.arrow!!)
357→        element is KtDestructuringDeclaration -> containsLineBreakInLeavesRange(element.lPar!!, element.rPar!!)
358→        element is KtValueArgumentList && element.children.size == 1 && element.anyDescendantOfType<KtCollectionLiteralExpression>() -> {
359→            // special handling for collection literal
360→            // @Annotation([
361→            //    "something",
362→            // ])
363→            val lastChild = element.collectDescendantsOfType<KtCollectionLiteralExpression>().last()
364→            containsLineBreakInLeavesRange(lastChild.rightBracket!!, element.rightParenthesis!!)
365→        }
366→        element is KtParameterList && element.parameters.isEmpty() -> false
367→        else -> element.textContains('\n')
368→    }
369→
370→    private fun ASTNode.addNewLineBeforeArrowInWhen() =
371→        if (psi is KtWhenEntry) {
372→            val leafBeforeArrow = (psi as KtWhenEntry).arrow?.prevLeaf()
373→            !(leafBeforeArrow is PsiWhiteSpace && leafBeforeArrow.textContains('\n'))
374→        } else {
375→            false
376→        }
377→
378→    private fun ASTNode.findPreviousTrailingCommaNodeOrNull(): ASTNode? {
379→        val codeLeaf = if (isCodeLeaf()) {
380→            this
381→        } else {
382→            prevCodeLeaf()
383→        }
384→        return codeLeaf?.takeIf { it.elementType == ElementType.COMMA }
385→    }
386→
387→    private fun containsLineBreakInLeavesRange(from: PsiElement, to: PsiElement): Boolean {
388→        var leaf: PsiElement? = from
389→        while (leaf != null && !leaf.isEquivalentTo(to)) {
390→            if (leaf.textContains('\n')) {
391→                return true
392→            }
393→            leaf = leaf.nextLeaf(skipEmptyElements = false)
394→        }
395→        return leaf?.textContains('\n') ?: false
396→    }
397→
398→    private enum class TrailingCommaState {
399→        /**
400→         * The trailing comma is needed and exists
401→         */
402→        EXISTS,
403→
404→        /**
405→         * The trailing comma is needed and doesn't exist
406→         */
407→        MISSING,
408→
409→        /**
410→         * The trailing comma isn't needed and doesn't exist
411→         */
412→        NOT_EXISTS,
413→
414→        /**
415→         * The trailing comma isn't needed, but exists
416→         */
417→        REDUNDANT,
418→    }
419→
420→    public companion object {
421→        private val BOOLEAN_VALUES_SET = setOf("true", "false")
422→
423→        public val TRAILING_COMMA_ON_DECLARATION_SITE_PROPERTY: EditorConfigProperty<Boolean> =
424→            EditorConfigProperty(
425→                type = PropertyType.LowerCasingPropertyType(
426→                    "ij_kotlin_allow_trailing_comma",
427→                    "Defines whether a trailing comma (or no trailing comma) should be enforced on the defining " +
428→                        "side, e.g. parameter-list, type-argument-list, lambda-value-parameters, enum-entries, etc." +
429→                        "When set, IntelliJ IDEA uses this property to allow usage of a trailing comma by discretion " +
430→                        "of the developer. KtLint however uses this setting to enforce consistent usage of the " +
431→                        "trailing comma when set.",
432→                    PropertyValueParser.BOOLEAN_VALUE_PARSER,
433→                    BOOLEAN_VALUES_SET,
434→                ),
435→                defaultValue = true,
436→                defaultAndroidValue = false,
437→            )
438→
439→        @Deprecated(
440→            message = "Marked for removal in KtLint 0.49",
441→            replaceWith = ReplaceWith("TRAILING_COMMA_ON_DECLARATION_SITE_PROPERTY"),
442→        )
443→        @Suppress("ktlint:experimental:property-naming")
444→        public val allowTrailingCommaProperty: EditorConfigProperty<Boolean> =
445→            TRAILING_COMMA_ON_DECLARATION_SITE_PROPERTY
446→
447→        private val TYPES_ON_DECLARATION_SITE = TokenSet.create(
448→            CLASS,
449→            DESTRUCTURING_DECLARATION,
450→            FUNCTION_LITERAL,
451→            FUNCTION_TYPE,
452→            TYPE_PARAMETER_LIST,
453→            VALUE_PARAMETER_LIST,
454→            WHEN_ENTRY,
455→        )
456→    }
457→}
458→

1→# How to make new project release
 2→
 3→### Publishing a new release
 4→
 5→Note: These steps should be done directly in the pinterest/ktlint repository, not in your personal fork.
 6→
 7→1. Create a new branch (e.g. `0.50.0-prep`)
 8→2. Update `VERSION_NAME` with the new release version in the project root `gradle.properties`. Be sure to remove `-SNAPSHOT`.
 9→3. Update `CHANGELOG.md` to rename the `Unreleased` section to the new release name, following the `## [x.x.x] - YYYY-MM-DD` format.
10→4. Add the new release to the bottom of the `CHANGELOG.md` file.
11→5. Push your changes to the branch, and merge it to `master`.
12→6. Update your local `pinterest/ktlint` `master` branch; verify you see the `gradle.properties` and `CHANGELOG.md` changes locally.
13→7. Add a tag with the new release version, and push it directly to remote (e.g. `git tag 0.50.0 && git push origin 0.50.0`). This will kick off the [Release workflow](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/actions/workflows/release.yml).
14→9. Close and release the repo on Sonatype. (Only Pinterest employees can do this.)
15→10. Find the `<release>-update-refs` branch in the repo (created by the `.announce` script) and merge it.
16→11. Update `gradle.properties` with the new `SNAPSHOT` version, and add a new empty `Unreleased` section to the top of `CHANGELOG.md` and commit. (This can be done directly in the main repo or in your fork.)
17→

**Evaluating semicolon tests**

I need to figure out why existing tests for semicolons are passing. I suspect they only check the formatting produced by the rule, rather than the entire standard ruleset. The implementation moves the semicolon to its own line, and the inspectNode is checking for the semicolon in lastEnumEntry.children(). It seems like this could work in their test cases, but a bug shows it fails with member declarations and in cases like `TestEnum()`. I wonder if the semicolon is treated differently depending on whether there's a member declaration.**Analyzing semicolon behavior**

It's quite plausible that in PSI, the semicolon is part of the enum entry when there are no members. However, if there are members, the semicolon might belong to a different node, possibly `ENUM_ENTRY_LIST` or `CLASS_BODY`. This seems logical: the semicolon could be a child of the enum entries list node. In scenarios where there is a declaration, the parser may represent the semicolon as a `SEMICOLON` leaf of `CLASS_BODY`. Interestingly, the test using `fun print()` after the semicolon passes, so it should cover the scenario we're concerned about, yet there's a difference as it lacks a companion object and has no empty constructor.**Investigating semicolon issues**

I'm pondering why the bug still arises, particularly with the primary constructor `TestEnum()`. Their Issue 1609 test includes a constructor with a parameter list and has a semicolon on the same line as `BAR("bar");`, plus a blank line before `fun doSomething`. So, that should cover cases with constructors too. The problem seems to involve a companion object within the enum class, which might impact the semicolon node location. It could be related to being in `CLASS_BODY` with `OBJECT_DECLARATION`. Yet, the tests for other declarations look similar. Interestingly, the error message points to a missing trailing comma before "}", not before the semicolon.**Clarifying semicolon identification**

It seems the rule has identified `}` as the inspectNode, which means the semicolon wasn't returned by `findNodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition`. This could happen when `semicolonAfterLastEnumEntry` is null, suggesting that `lastChildNode` is likely `}` when the class has declarations. But why would `semicolonAfterLastEnumEntry` be null? It searches for the semicolon in `lastEnumEntry.children()`, which would return null if the semicolon isn't a child. Perhaps it's attached to the previous enum entry instead. In the tests, it seems to be treated as a child, so that works. However, in the reported issue, the semicolon isn't a child, and I wonder in which AST it becomes a sibling. Is it when the last enum entry has a constructor call or a body? In the example, the enum entry is simply `B;`, yet it appears the semicolon could be a sibling. I need to replicate this by parsing to inspect PSI.**Improving semicolon handling**

I think we can utilize Kotlin PSI in tests, but it would be easier to create a robust function that finds the semicolon as both a child and a sibling of the enum entry. This would address the issues at hand. I'll adjust `findNodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition` to search for the semicolon starting with `lastEnumEntry` among its children, and if not found, check the next leaf in the class body, ignoring whitespace and comments.

If we encounter a semicolon after comments, we might need to return the comment instead. Additionally, we may need to adjust the autocorrect logic, as it currently assumes the semicolon is always a child and could fail if it's a sibling. In the scenario where the semicolon is a sibling, we want to ensure it’s moved to its own line.

Looking at the existing code for `lastEnumEntry`, if the semicolon is a sibling, we also need to maintain proper formatting for future use, particularly regarding indentation after inserting a comma. I’ll implement this logic to ensure semicolons are always placed correctly, especially when following entries with commas. This fix should clarify the issues and correctly handle formatting in any scenario related to enum entries.**Refining semicolon handling in code**

I’m thinking about how to improve the handling of semicolons after adding a comma. After insertion, I should check if there’s a newline between the comma and the semicolon. While existing tests expect semicolons on a new line, I’m unsure if this is necessary for the bug, though it seems the tests align with IntelliJ’s formatting rules. 

Given our changes, the tests might fail since they currently pass with semicolons as children. Running tests should help clarify semicolon representation in these contexts. I’ll look into running gradle tests to mimic the behavior. It appears the updated method should accurately find a sibling semicolon, and fixes should maintain the expected formatting. This strategy should prevent any violations while inserting commas correctly in the AST, ensuring stability and alignment with expected outcomes.**Optimizing comma and semicolon handling**

I'm realizing that although the file appears correct, the trailing comma isn't positioned properly in the AST representation despite being visible in text. This could create inconsistencies with the no-semi rule. I suspect the comma insertion uses an incorrect AST manipulation method. 

For enum entries, it's crucial to ensure that the comma is inserted as a sibling rather than as a child of the identifier. In fact, the comma should be placed after the enum entry itself. For the tests involving comments, the semicolon needs to be moved to a new line after inserting the comma, ensuring proper formatting. It looks like I need to address this formatting issue in the code execution pathway for enum entries.**Correcting comma and semicolon positioning**

I’m analyzing how the whitespace and indentation are created when handling enum entries. It looks like I’m adding a newline and removing the semicolon from the enum entry, which leads to the insertion of the comma after the last identifier. However, if the comma is contained within the enum entry, it may affect the intended structure of the AST. 

To fix this, I need to ensure that commas are added as siblings rather than children of the enum entry, especially when comments are involved. I’ll explore updating the insertion logic accordingly.**Adjusting comma and semicolon handling**

I’m planning to modify the code for comma insertion. The goal is to check if the current node is a semicolon within a class body. If so, I should add the comma before that semicolon. However, I need to ensure that comments are properly handled. If a comment exists after the semicolon, I should place the comma before the semicolon to keep it correctly positioned.

To test this, I'll consider running KtLint to check for formatting issues and potentially use KtLintAssertThat for asserting no violations exist after the changes.

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.test
  2→
  3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.LintError

 11→import org.assertj.core.api.AbstractAssert
 12→import org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat

 15→/**
 16→ * AssertJ style assertion for verifying KtLint rules. This class is intended to be used as follows:
 17→ *
 18→ * ```
 19→ * // Create an assertThat assertion for a specific rule or set of rules. If possible define it at the class level
 20→ * val someRuleAssertThat = SomeRule().assertThat()
 21→ *
 22→ * @Test
 23→ * fun `Some test`() {
 24→ *     val code = .... // Original code to be formatted
 25→ *     val formattedCode = .... // The code as it should be formatted
 26→ *     someRuleAssertThat(code)
 27→ *         .hasLintViolation(1, 1, "some-error-description")
 28→ *         .isFormattedAs(formattedCode)
 29→ * }
 30→ * ```
 31→ */
 32→@Suppress("MemberVisibilityCanBePrivate")
 33→public class KtLintAssertThat(
 34→    /**
 35→     * Provider of a rule which is the subject of the test, e.g. the rule for which the AssertThat is created.
 36→     */
 37→    private val ruleProvider: RuleProvider,
 38→    /**
 39→     * The code which is to be linted and formatted.
 40→     */
 41→    private val code: String,
 42→    /**
 43→     * Providers of rules which have to be executed in addition to the main rule when linting/formatting the code. Note
 44→     * that lint errors for those rules are suppressed.
 45→     */
 46→    private val additionalRuleProviders: MutableSet<RuleProvider>,
 47→) {
 48→    private var filePath: String? = null
 49→    private var kotlinScript = false
 50→    private var editorConfigProperties = emptySet<Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>>()
 51→
 52→    /**
 53→     * Set the [EditorConfigOverride] properties to be used by the rule. This function can be called multiple times.
 54→     * Properties which have been set before, are silently overwritten with the new vale.
 55→     */
 56→    public fun withEditorConfigOverride(
 57→        vararg properties: Pair<EditorConfigProperty<*>, *>,
 58→    ): KtLintAssertThat {
 59→        editorConfigProperties = editorConfigProperties + properties.toSet()
 60→
 61→        return this
 62→    }
 63→
 64→    /**
 65→     * Set the [EditorConfigOverride] "max_line_length" property based on the EOL Marker which is places at the first
 66→     * line of the code sample. If the property has been set before via [withEditorConfigOverride] then that value is
 67→     * silently overwritten.
 68→     *
 69→     * Example of usage:
 70→     * ```
 71→     *  val code =
 72→     *      """
 73→     *      // $MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER                   $EOL_CHAR
 74→     *      val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
 75→     *      """.trimIndent()
 76→     *  maxLineLengthRuleAssertThat(code)
 77→     *      .setMaxLineLength()
 78→     *      .hasLintViolation(2, 1, "Exceeded max line length (46)")
 79→     * ```
 80→     */
 81→    @Throws(MissingEolMarker::class)
 82→    public fun setMaxLineLength(): KtLintAssertThat {
 83→        code
 84→            .split("\n")
 85→            .firstOrNull { it.contains(MAX_LINE_LENGTH_MARKER) && it.endsWith(EOL_CHAR) }
 86→            ?.indexOf(EOL_CHAR)
 87→            ?.let { index ->
 88→                editorConfigProperties =
 89→                    editorConfigProperties + setOf(MAX_LINE_LENGTH_PROPERTY to (index + 1).toString())
 90→            } ?: throw MissingEolMarker()
 91→
 92→        return this
 93→    }
 94→
 95→    /**
 96→     * Handle the code as if it was specified in file on the given path.
 97→     */
 98→    public fun asFileWithPath(filePath: String): KtLintAssertThat {
 99→        this.filePath = filePath
100→        return this
101→    }
102→
103→    /**
104→     * Handle the code as Kotlin script (kts).
105→     */
106→    public fun asKotlinScript(kotlinScript: Boolean = true): KtLintAssertThat {
107→        this.kotlinScript = kotlinScript
108→        return this
109→    }
110→
111→    /**
112→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used
113→     * to unit test rules which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and
114→     * indenting.
115→     *
116→     * Caution:
117→     * An additional rule provider for a rule which actually is executed before the rule under test, might result in
118→     * unexpected [LintViolation] in case that additional rule is modifying the AST. During the [lint] phase, all rules
119→     * are executed in parallel and as of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same
120→     * version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST,
121→     * this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is executed later than the additional rule.
122→     *
123→     * Prefer to use [addAdditionalRuleProviders] when adding multiple providers of rules.
124→     */
125→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProvider(provider: () -> Rule): KtLintAssertThat {
126→        additionalRuleProviders.add(RuleProvider(provider))
127→
128→        return this
129→    }
130→
131→    /**
132→     * Adds a single provider of an additional rule to be executed when linting/formatting the code. This can to be used
133→     * to unit test rules which are best to be tested in conjunction with other rules, for example wrapping and

139→     * are executed in parallel and as of that both the rule under test and the additional rule are using the exact same
140→     * version of the AST. When, in the [format] stage the additional rule is run first, and if it modifies the AST,
141→     * this could result in a [LintViolation] for the rule under test which is executed later than the additional rule.
142→     *

144→     */
145→    public fun addAdditionalRuleProviders(vararg providers: (() -> Rule)): KtLintAssertThat {
146→        additionalRuleProviders.addAll(

150→        return this
151→    }
152→
153→    /**
154→     * Asserts that the code does not contain any [LintViolation]s in the rule associated with the KtLintAssertThat.
155→     *

158→    public fun hasNoLintViolations(): Unit = ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolations()
159→
160→    /**

162→     *
163→     * Note: When linting succeeds without errors, formatting is also checked.

165→    public fun hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules(): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =
166→        ktLintAssertThatAssertable().hasNoLintViolationsExceptInAdditionalRules()
167→
168→    /**
169→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] which automatically can be corrected. This is a sugar
170→     * coated version of [hasLintViolations] for the case that the code contains exactly one lint violation.
171→     */
172→    public fun hasLintViolation(

178→
179→    /**

181→     * resulting in only one (type of) [LintViolation] are usually easier to comprehend.
182→     */
183→    public fun hasLintViolations(vararg expectedErrors: LintViolation): KtLintAssertThatAssertable =

185→
186→    /**
187→     * Asserts that the code does contain given [LintViolation] caused by an additional rule which automatically can be

300→public class KtLintAssertThatAssertable(

315→    /**

326→
327→        assertAll(

329→                assertThat(lintErrorsWhenFormatting)

332→                            if (additionalRuleProviders.isEmpty()) {

336→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."
337→                            },

339→                    .isEmpty()
340→            },
341→            {
342→                assertThat(actualFormattedCode)

347→                            } else {
348→                                "If this is caused by an additional rule added to the test, then remove that rule in case it " +
349→                                    "is always executed before the rule under test."

**Refining testing approach**

It seems better to directly call `setOf(RuleProvider { ... }, ...).format()` in the test. This way, I can assert that the lint errors list is empty and that the formatted code matches what's expected. That might be the easiest method. I wonder if there are extension functions for `.format` available in the test utilities? I think it's in the ktlint-test module, so I’ll search for the `.format(` extension to confirm.

content.thinking:
**Analyzing KtLint behavior**

I'm diving into the documentation for ktlint. I suspect that `--format` may return 0 if everything is formatted correctly, but 1 if there are remaining errors that can't be autocorrected. 

It seems like the actual bug isn't that corrected errors are reported, but that they aren't marked as corrected due to an issue with AST insertion. I'll need to ensure that the lintErrors list is empty after formatting or confirm that any existing errors were corrected. However, it looks like the engine reports lint errors no matter what!**Inspecting KtLint callback behavior**

I’m thinking about how the callback handles lint errors. It seems to capture both lintError and whether it was corrected, but it might still add errors even when corrections are true. They seem to expect the list to be empty after successful linting, yet there are no errors detected even on clean code. 

I need to investigate KtLintRuleEngine.format's callback contract to confirm if it only handles uncorrected errors. If corrected errors aren't passed, that makes the corrected boolean seem a bit unnecessary. I'll quickly inspect the implementation by searching for `format(` in the core code.

content.thinking:
**Updating node function**

I think our classBody.findNodeAfterTrailingCommaPosition might be returning the lastChildNode because it can’t find a semicolon. So, it seems we should update the function to locate the semicolon sibling through leaf traversal. I’ll make that targeted change to find it after the last enum entry while keeping the previous child lookup for cases where the semicolon is a child. We must also be cautious not to return a semicolon inside the companion object body or traverse further than necessary. That feels right!**Editing file for enum behavior**

I see that if the next leaf is `companion`, we should return that instead of rbrace. However, this could change the behavior for invalid enums missing a semicolon, especially if a trailing comma is inserted before the companion. That might affect existing tests, but I think it’s worth the consideration. For valid enums with members and a semicolon, the next leaf should indeed be the semicolon. So, I’ll proceed with editing the file between lines 221-229 using the edit tool!

561→                    }
562→            } catch (e: RuleExecutionException) {
563→                throw e.toKtLintRuleException(code.fileName)
564→            }
565→        }

567→        errors
568→            .sortedWith { (l), (r) -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
569→            .forEach { (e, corrected) -> callback(e, corrected) }
570→
571→        if (!mutated) {
572→            return code.content
573→        }
574→
575→        val formattedCode = ruleExecutionContext
576→            .rootNode
577→            .text
578→            .replace("\n", ruleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(code.content))
579→        return if (hasUTF8BOM) {
580→            UTF8_BOM + formattedCode
581→        } else {
582→            formattedCode
583→        }.also {
584→            LOGGER.debug("Finished with formatting file '${code.fileName}'")
585→        }
586→    }

588→    private fun RuleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(fileContent: String): String {
589→        val eol =
590→            editorConfigProperties["end_of_line"]
591→                ?.sourceValue
592→        return when {
593→            eol == "native" -> System.lineSeparator()
594→            eol == "crlf" || eol != "lf" && fileContent.lastIndexOf('\r') != -1 -> "\r\n"
595→            else -> "\n"
596→        }

603→     * [KtLintRuleEngineConfiguration] for missing properties default values, generates Kotlin section (default is
604→     * `[*.{kt,kts}]`) new content.
605→     *
606→     * @return Kotlin section editorconfig content. For example:
607→     * ```properties
608→     * final-newline=true
609→     * indent-size=4
610→     * ```
611→     */
612→    public fun generateKotlinEditorConfigSection(filePath: Path): String {
613→        val codeStyle =
614→            editorConfigOverride
615→                .properties[CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY]
616→                ?.parsed
617→                ?.safeAs<CodeStyleValue>()
618→                ?: CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY.defaultValue
619→        val rules =
620→            ruleProviders
621→                .map { RuleRunner(it) }
622→                .distinctBy { it.ruleId }
623→                .toSet()
624→                .map { it.getRule() }
625→                .toSet()
626→        return EditorConfigGenerator(this.editorConfigLoader).generateEditorconfig(
627→            filePath,
628→            rules,
629→            codeStyle,
630→        )
631→    }

 4→ * Provides a [Rule] instance. Important: to ensure that a [Rule] can keep internal state and that processing of files
 5→ * is thread-safe, a *new* instance should be provided on each call of [createNewRuleInstance]. A custom [RuleProvider]
 6→ * does not need to take care of clearing internal state as KtLint calls this method any time the [Rule] instance has
 7→ * been used for processing a file and as of that might have an internal state set.
 8→ */
 9→public class RuleProvider(
10→    /**
11→     * Lambda which creates a new instance of the rule.
12→     */
13→    private val provider: () -> Rule,
14→) {
15→    public fun createNewRuleInstance(): Rule =
16→        provider()
17→}

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.core.internal
  2→
  3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule
  4→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.EditorConfigProperties
  5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.UsesEditorConfigProperties
  6→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY
  7→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.EditorConfigProperty
  8→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.KTLINT_DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY
  9→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.RULE_EXECUTION_PROPERTY_TYPE
 10→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.RuleExecution
 11→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.createRuleExecutionEditorConfigProperty
 12→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.ktLintRuleExecutionPropertyName
 13→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.api.editorconfig.ktLintRuleSetExecutionPropertyName
 14→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.initKtLintKLogger
 15→import mu.KotlinLogging
 16→
 17→private val LOGGER = KotlinLogging.logger {}.initKtLintKLogger()
 18→
 19→/**
 20→ * The VisitorProvider is created for each file being scanned. As the [RuleRunnerSorter] logs the order in which the
 21→ * rules are executed, a singleton instance of the class is used to prevent that the logs are flooded with duplicate
 22→ * log lines.
 23→ */
 24→private val RULE_RUNNER_SORTER = RuleRunnerSorter()
 25→
 26→internal class VisitorProvider(
 27→    ruleRunners: Set<RuleRunner>,
 28→    /**
 29→     * Creates a new [RuleRunnerSorter]. Only to be used in unit tests where the same set of rules are used with distinct [Rule.VisitorModifier]s.
 30→     */
 31→    recreateRuleSorter: Boolean = false,
 32→) : UsesEditorConfigProperties {
 33→    override val editorConfigProperties: List<EditorConfigProperty<*>> = listOf(
 34→        KTLINT_DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY,
 35→        DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY,
 36→    ).plus(
 37→        ruleRunners.map {
 38→            createRuleExecutionEditorConfigProperty(toQualifiedRuleId(it.ruleSetId, it.ruleId))
 39→        },
 40→    )
 41→
 42→    /**
 43→     * The list of [ruleRunnersSorted] is sorted based on the [Rule.VisitorModifier] of the rules.
 44→     */
 45→    private val ruleRunnersSorted: List<RuleRunner> =
 46→        if (recreateRuleSorter) {
 47→            RuleRunnerSorter()
 48→        } else {
 49→            RULE_RUNNER_SORTER
 50→        }.getSortedRuleRunners(ruleRunners)
 51→
 52→    internal fun visitor(editorConfigProperties: EditorConfigProperties): ((rule: Rule, fqRuleId: String) -> Unit) -> Unit {
 53→        val enabledRuleRunners =
 54→            ruleRunnersSorted
 55→                .filter { ruleRunner -> isEnabled(editorConfigProperties, ruleRunner.qualifiedRuleId) }
 56→        if (enabledRuleRunners.isEmpty()) {
 57→            LOGGER.debug { "Skipping file as no enabled rules are found to be executed" }
 58→            return { _ -> }
 59→        }
 60→        val ruleRunnersToBeSkipped =
 61→            ruleRunnersSorted
 62→                .filter { ruleRunner ->
 63→                    val runAfterRules = ruleRunner.runAfterRules
 64→                    val runAfterRuleIds = runAfterRules.map { it.ruleId.toQualifiedRuleId() }
 65→                    runAfterRules
 66→                        .any {
 67→                            it.runOnlyWhenOtherRuleIsEnabled &&
 68→                                enabledRuleRunners.none { it.qualifiedRuleId in runAfterRuleIds }
 69→                        }
 70→                }
 71→        if (ruleRunnersToBeSkipped.isNotEmpty()) {
 72→            LOGGER.debug {
 73→                ruleRunnersToBeSkipped
 74→                    .forEach {
 75→                        println(
 76→                            "Skipping rule with id '${it.qualifiedRuleId}'. This rule has to run after rules with " +
 77→                                "ids '${it.runAfterRules.joinToString { it.ruleId.toQualifiedRuleId() }}' and will " +
 78→                                "not run in case that rule is disabled.",
 79→                        )
 80→                    }
 81→            }
 82→        }
 83→        val ruleRunnersToExecute = enabledRuleRunners - ruleRunnersToBeSkipped.toSet()
 84→        if (ruleRunnersToExecute.isEmpty()) {
 85→            LOGGER.debug { "Skipping file as no enabled rules are found to be executed" }
 86→            return { _ -> }
 87→        }
 88→        return { visit ->
 89→            ruleRunnersToExecute.forEach {
 90→                visit(it.getRule(), it.shortenedQualifiedRuleId)
 91→            }
 92→        }
 93→    }
 94→
 95→    private fun isEnabled(editorConfigProperties: EditorConfigProperties, qualifiedRuleId: String): Boolean =
 96→        // For backwards compatibility all different properties which affects enabling/disabling of properties have to
 97→        // be checked. Note that properties are checked in order of precedence. If a property of higher precedence has
 98→        // been defined, all properties with lower precedence are ignore entirely.
 99→        when {
100→            editorConfigProperties.containsKey(ktLintRuleExecutionPropertyName(qualifiedRuleId)) ||
101→                editorConfigProperties.containsKey(ktLintRuleSetExecutionPropertyName(qualifiedRuleId)) ->
102→                editorConfigProperties.isRuleEnabled(qualifiedRuleId)
103→
104→            editorConfigProperties.containsKey(KTLINT_DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY.name) ->
105→                editorConfigProperties.isEnabled(
106→                    KTLINT_DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY,
107→                    qualifiedRuleId,
108→                ) && ruleSetId(qualifiedRuleId) != "experimental"
109→
110→            editorConfigProperties.containsKey(DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY.name) ->
111→                editorConfigProperties.isEnabled(
112→                    DISABLED_RULES_PROPERTY,
113→                    qualifiedRuleId,
114→                ) && ruleSetId(qualifiedRuleId) != "experimental"
115→
116→            else ->
117→                ruleSetId(qualifiedRuleId) != "experimental"
118→        }
119→
120→    private fun EditorConfigProperties.isRuleEnabled(qualifiedRuleId: String) =

conversation_history.content:
1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.core.internal
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule
 4→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule.VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule
 5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.RuleProvider
 6→
 7→// TODO: Rename to RuleInstanceProvider. Also rename RuleRunnerSorter.
 8→internal class RuleRunner(private val provider: RuleProvider) {
 9→    private var rule = provider.createNewRuleInstance()
10→
11→    val qualifiedRuleId = rule.toQualifiedRuleId()
12→    val shortenedQualifiedRuleId = qualifiedRuleId.removePrefix("standard:")
13→
14→    val ruleId = rule.id
15→    val ruleSetId = qualifiedRuleId.substringBefore(':')
16→
17→    val runAsLateAsPossible = rule.visitorModifiers.contains(Rule.VisitorModifier.RunAsLateAsPossible)
18→    var runAfterRules = setRunAfterRules()
19→
20→    /**
21→     * Gets the [Rule]. If the [Rule] has already been used for traversal of the AST, a new instance of the [Rule] is
22→     * provided. This prevents leakage of the state of the Rule between executions.
23→     */
24→    fun getRule(): Rule {
25→        if (rule.isUsedForTraversalOfAST()) {
26→            rule = provider.createNewRuleInstance()
27→        }
28→        return rule
29→    }
30→
31→    private fun setRunAfterRules(): List<RunAfterRule> =
32→        rule
33→            .visitorModifiers
34→            .filterIsInstance<RunAfterRule>()
35→            .map {
36→                val runAfterRuleVisitorModifier = it as RunAfterRule
37→                val qualifiedAfterRuleId = runAfterRuleVisitorModifier.ruleId.toQualifiedRuleId()
38→                check(qualifiedRuleId != qualifiedAfterRuleId) {
39→                    // Do not print the fully qualified rule id in the error message as it might not appear in the code
40→                    // in case it is a rule from the 'standard' rule set.
41→                    "Rule with id '${rule.id}' has a visitor modifier of type '${RunAfterRule::class.simpleName}' " +
42→                        "but it is not referring to another rule but to the rule itself. A rule can not run after " +
43→                        "itself. This should be fixed by the maintainer of the rule."
44→                }
45→                runAfterRuleVisitorModifier.copy(
46→                    ruleId = qualifiedAfterRuleId,
47→                )
48→            }
49→
50→    fun removeRunAfterRules(runAfterRules: Set<RunAfterRule>) {
51→        require(!rule.isUsedForTraversalOfAST()) {
52→            "RunAfterRules can not be removed when RuleRunner has already been used for traversal of the AST"
53→        }
54→        this.runAfterRules = this.runAfterRules - runAfterRules
55→    }
56→}
57→

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.core.internal
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.core.Rule
 4→
 5→internal fun Rule.toQualifiedRuleId() =
 6→    id.toQualifiedRuleId()
 7→
 8→internal fun String.toQualifiedRuleId(): String {
 9→    val ruleId = if (contains(":")) {
10→        this
11→    } else {
12→        "standard:$this"
13→    }
14→    // When IntelliJ IDEA is reformatting the ".editorconfig" file it sometimes add a space after the comma in a
15→    // comma-separate-list which should not be a part of the ruleId
16→    return ruleId.removeSpaces()
17→}
18→
19→internal fun toQualifiedRuleId(
20→    ruleSetId: String,
21→    ruleId: String,
22→): String {
23→    if (ruleSetId.contains(" ") || ruleId.contains(" ")) {
24→        return toQualifiedRuleId(ruleSetId.removeSpaces(), ruleId.removeSpaces())
25→    }
26→    return if (ruleSetId == "" || ruleId.startsWith("$ruleSetId:")) {
27→        ruleId.toQualifiedRuleId()
28→    } else {
29→        "$ruleSetId:$ruleId"
30→    }
31→}
32→
33→private fun String.removeSpaces() =
34→    this.replace(" ", "")
35→
36→internal fun ruleId(qualifiedRuleId: String) =
37→    qualifiedRuleId.substringAfter(":", qualifiedRuleId)
38→
39→internal fun ruleSetId(qualifiedRuleId: String) =
40→    qualifiedRuleId.substringBefore(":", "standard")

content.thinking:
**Examining linting rules**

It looks like there was a trailing comma error that got fixed, but now the "no-semi" rule is incorrectly flagging semicolons after enum entries when there are class members. It seems necessary to check the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) to see if it reflects this structure properly. I'm curious whether the semicolon after fixing the trailing comma has been moved to a new line. I should delve into the NoSemicolonsRule logic to understand its behavior in these cases.

content.thinking:
**Evaluating NoSemicolonsRule adjustments**

I'm looking to fix the NoSemicolonsRule by extending its logic to recognize semicolons related to enum entries, even when they’re not directly under the enum entry. If a semicolon is found in the class body but is preceded by an enum entry, it should still be seen as required. There's an inconsistency since currently, it flags incorrectly in certain cases. To solve this, I’ll need to ensure that when repositioning semicolons, it properly recognizes them as children of ENUM_ENTRY.**Analyzing the trailing comma rule**

I'm noticing that the current trailing comma rule seems to move semicolons from the enum entry to the parent, making it a sibling, which the no-semi rule doesn't handle well. There's a comment indicating that semicolons should be children of the enum entry, which suggests they shouldn’t be moved. Yet, the code under a specific condition moves them to the parent, making the rule seem contradictory. It ends up treating the semicolon as unnecessary, even though it’s actually required. I need to understand why they would move it out like this.**Refining semicolon handling**

I think the formatting changes made were likely to put the semicolon on a separate line, but they also added a newline into the enum entry, which results in the semicolon appearing in the parent after that newline. They could have left it as a child instead. It seems that if they intended to move the semicolon, the no-semi rule should have been updated accordingly. To resolve our test failure, I’ll modify the NoSemicolonsRule to consider semicolons after enum entries as necessary, even if they’re siblings.**Clarifying semicolon requirements**

I need to specify the conditions under which a semicolon is required. First, if the node's element type is SEMICOLON, I should check if this node resides within CLASS_BODY. Then, I can look for a previous sibling code leaf that is an enum entry and a following code leaf that's not a right brace (rbrace). If all these conditions are met, the semicolon is necessary. However, I should avoid treating semicolons as required in other contexts.**Implementing semicolon logic**

I’m working on the `isNoSemicolonRequiredAfter` function. First, I'm checking if the previous code leaf exists; if not, I return true. Then, I need to handle cases where the node's parent is an ENUM_ENTRY or CLASS_BODY. If it's in a CLASS_BODY and the previous leaf is an enum entry, this is crucial because I want to confirm that a semicolon is necessary when it separates enum entries from class declarations. 

Since whitespace and comments can affect the next leaf retrieval, I need to ensure that any declaration following the semicolon isn’t a right brace. I’ll use the existing helper `doesNotRequirePreSemi` to skip those unnecessary nodes. This decision is essential to ensure I don’t mistakenly label the semicolon as unnecessary.

content.thinking:
**Summarizing code fixes**

All module tests have passed, which is great! I need to double-check that my fix for the TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRule didn't introduce extra warnings. There’s a previous warning about an unused variable in the NoSemicolonsRule, but that seems unrelated to my changes. My modifications ensured that formatting enums with companion objects won't produce violations. I’ll also confirm that the no-semicolon fix hasn’t impacted other scenarios, but since the tests passed, everything looks good!

content.text:
Implemented a fix for the “first run fails, second run succeeds” enum formatting bug:

- **`TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRule`**: made detection of the enum-entry terminating semicolon robust by also handling the case where the semicolon is a **sibling in the class body** (not a child of the last enum entry).
- **`NoSemicolonsRule`**: prevented a false-positive “Unnecessary semicolon” when the enum-entry separator semicolon is (temporarily) positioned under the **class body** during formatting, by treating it as **required** when declarations follow.

Added a new regression test:
- `TrailingCommaOnDeclarationSiteRuleEnumWithCompanionObjectRegressionTest.kt`

All `:ktlint-ruleset-standard:test` tests pass after the change.
